title: Wallet-to-Wallet Crypto Swap: How the Route Works Before You Send
keyQuestions: What is a wallet-to-wallet crypto swap?
How does a wallet-to-wallet swap differ from an exchange account balance?
What should users prepare before sending from a source wallet?
Why does the receiving address matter in a wallet-to-wallet route?
How do fixed-rate and floating-rate quotes fit into wallet-to-wallet swaps?
What should users do if a wallet-to-wallet swap status is unclear?
quickSteps: Prepare the source wallet with the correct asset, network, and fee before opening the swap route.
Prepare the destination wallet and copy the receiving address from the current wallet, not from old notes or chats.
Review the quote, rate model, amount, minimum, network, and deposit instructions before sending funds.
Track the transaction by status and transaction ID after sending instead of reacting to every delay.
Stop and verify the route if the asset, network, amount, address, or status does not match the expected wallet-to-wallet path.

issueRouting.start: The user wants to swap crypto directly from a source wallet to a destination wallet and needs to understand the route before sending funds.

branches.if: The source wallet is not ready
then: Prepare the correct asset and network before opening the route.
Check whether the wallet has enough fee balance.
Create or review the quote only after the wallet can send cleanly.

branches.if: The receiving wallet or address is unclear
then: Copy the receiving address from the current destination wallet.
Verify the asset, network, and any memo or tag requirements before continuing.

branches.if: The swap status looks unclear after sending
then: Do not send a second transaction immediately.
Check the transaction ID, network, amount, confirmation status, and visible route status.
Share only narrow transaction facts with support if review is needed.

riskNotes: A wallet-to-wallet swap depends on the exact transaction facts broadcast from the source wallet.
A correct quote cannot fix a wrong receiving address, wrong network, wrong asset, or below-minimum deposit.
The same token ticker can exist on different networks, so network selection must match the route.
Wallet fee behavior can change the actual amount sent and should be checked before confirmation.
A pending source transaction is not always broken; confirmation timing and route detection should be reviewed before taking another action.
Support requests should use TXID, chain, amount, time sent, and visible status, not seed phrases, private keys, wallet backups, or remote access.
